Canvas How-To Guide

The Complete AoL Workflow in Canvas

Four parts covering the full process: setting up outcomes, adding them to rubrics, making attainment judgements at the point of marking, and exporting data for your program team.

This guide covers the complete Canvas workflow for AoL data collection, from setting up outcomes through to exporting the data for your program team. Use the tabs below to move between parts. You can return to any part at any time.

1
Navigate to Outcomes
2
Name and Description
3
Proficiency Ratings
4
Calculation Method
5
Group and Create
Step 1

Navigate to Outcomes

From the left-hand navigation menu in your Canvas course, select Outcomes. This will open the Outcomes management page. To begin creating a new outcome, click the + Create button in the top right corner, highlighted below.

Step 2

Enter the CLO Name and Description

The Create Outcome dialogue will open with two fields at the top: Name and Friendly Name.

  • In the Name field, enter the CLO label, for example CLO 1. This identifier will appear in the rubric and in the Learning Mastery Pathway view.
  • Leave the Friendly Name field blank.
  • In the Description field, paste the full text of the CLO as it appears in your course outline.
  • Leave the Friendly description field blank.
Tip
The Description field is what students will see when they view their Learning Mastery results. Pasting the full CLO text here ensures students can see exactly which outcome is being tracked against each assignment.
Step 3

Set the Proficiency Ratings

Scrolling down reveals the Proficiency rating section. Canvas displays its own default ratings as shown below. These need to be replaced with the Business School's three-level scale.

Delete two of the five default rows using the bin icon, leaving three rows. Then rename and update the remaining rows to match exactly:

SettingValue
Exceeds expectations0.015
Meets expectations0.01
Doesn't meet expectations0
Mastery at0.01 points
Note
The point values are set deliberately low and precise. Canvas rounds point values to the nearest whole number in the rubric display. If higher values such as 5, 3, and 0 were used, those numbers would appear in the rubric row and students might assume they contribute to the assignment grade. Using 0.015, 0.01, and 0 ensures the outcome row displays no grade-contributing points, making it clear that this row tracks attainment only.
Step 4

Configure the Calculation Method

Below the proficiency ratings is the Calculation method section. This determines how Canvas calculates the final attainment level for an outcome when it has been assessed more than once across the course.

The default setting is Weighted Average at 65%, which means the most recent result contributes 65% of the final attainment score, with all previous results contributing the remaining 35%. Note that the 65% weighting is adjustable. If the final collection of CLO data is particularly significant in demonstrating full attainment, this weighting can be increased to reflect that importance.

Tip

The calculation method should reflect how the outcome is designed to progress across your assignments. Canvas offers five options:

Weighted Average (default, 65/35): best when the outcome is assessed multiple times and the most recent result should carry more weight, but all results contribute.

Decaying Average: gives progressively less weight to older results the more times the outcome is assessed. Good when earlier attempts are genuinely less representative of current capability.

n Number of Times: useful when a repeated skill is applied across different contexts rather than progressively developed, and you want to confirm consistent mastery across a set number of attempts.

Most Recent Score: always uses the most recent result. Suited to situations where only the final demonstration matters.

Highest Score: always uses the best result. Contact TLI if you are unsure which method fits your course design.

Note
The calculation method only applies when the outcome is assessed more than once. If an outcome appears in only one assignment, the single result is returned regardless of the method selected.
Step 5

Assign to a Group and Create

At the bottom of the Create Outcome dialogue is the Location section. Select the relevant group for your course, or create a new one if this is your first outcome. Once selected, the group will appear highlighted. Click Create to save the outcome.

Repeat Steps 2 to 5 for each CLO in your course.

Tip
Create all of your CLOs before moving on to Part B. Having all outcomes set up in advance makes the process of adding them to rubric criteria significantly more straightforward.
1
Open the Rubric
2
Map Criteria to CLOs
3
Used for Grading
4
Rubric Configurations
Step 1

Open the Assignment Rubric

Navigate to the assignment you want to add outcomes to and open its rubric for editing. If the assignment does not yet have a rubric, you will need to create one before proceeding. The rubric should already have criteria that reflect the marking structure of the assignment.

The goal of Part B is to attach a CLO underneath each relevant criterion, so that when you mark against that criterion, Canvas also records attainment for the associated outcome. The relationship between your criteria and your CLOs is the key design decision here, and Part B walks through the main configurations you will encounter.

Key
Before proceeding, make sure all CLOs for the course have been created in Canvas as described in Part A. You cannot attach an outcome to a rubric criterion unless the outcome already exists in the system.
Step 2

Map Criteria to CLOs

Each criterion in a rubric should be traceable to a CLO. The mapping question is: which CLO does this criterion provide evidence of? In most cases this will be straightforward, because well-designed criteria are derived from the CLOs themselves. Where a criterion clearly belongs to one CLO, that outcome is added directly beneath it. Where a criterion spans more than one CLO, a judgement call is needed about which outcome it most directly evidences.

To add an outcome, scroll to the bottom of the Criteria Builder in the rubric editor. Click Create From Outcome, highlighted below. This opens the Find outcome dialogue where you can browse or search for the CLO you want to attach.

In the Find outcome dialogue, select the relevant CLO from the list on the left. The right-hand panel shows the outcome details and mastery levels. Before clicking Import, check that the "Use this criterion for scoring" checkbox is unchecked. This is covered in Step 3 below. Click Import to add the outcome row to the rubric. Repeat this process for each CLO that needs to be mapped in the rubric.

Note
The point values set in Part A (0.015, 0.01, and 0) provide a safety net: even if "Use this criterion for scoring" is accidentally left on, the negligible values mean the grade impact is effectively zero. But it is best practice to uncheck it before importing, so nothing is missed.
Tip
Think of the CLO row not as a separate marking criterion but as a summary judgement that sits beneath the criterion or criteria it draws from. The criterion rows carry the marks; the CLO row carries the outcome attainment record.
Step 3

Uncheck "Used for Grading"

When you add an outcome to a rubric in Canvas, it appears as a row with its own point scale. By default, Canvas may attempt to include this row in the overall grade calculation. This is not what we want: the CLO row should track attainment only, without contributing marks to the student's grade.

To prevent this, locate the "Used for grading" checkbox or toggle for the outcome row and make sure it is unchecked. This is critical. If the outcome row contributes to grading, it will distort the assignment mark, and students will see confusing numbers in their grade breakdown.

Note
This is also the reason the point values were set to 0.015, 0.01, and 0 in Part A. Even if "Used for grading" is accidentally left on, the negligible values mean the grade impact is effectively zero. But it is best practice to uncheck it regardless.
Step 4

The Three Rubric Configurations

Most rubrics will fall into one of three configurations when it comes to mapping CLOs to criteria. Understanding which configuration applies to each CLO in your assignment will prepare you for the marking judgements described in Part C.

Configuration 1: One criterion mapped to one CLO

The simplest case. A single criterion in the rubric is the primary evidence for a CLO, and the outcome is placed directly beneath it. At the point of marking, the criterion score straightforwardly indicates whether the outcome has been exceeded, met, or not met. No aggregation is needed.

Configuration 2: Two or more criteria mapped to one CLO

Where a CLO is evidenced by multiple criteria, those criteria should ideally be grouped together in the rubric so the CLO row sits naturally beneath them. If reordering the criteria would disrupt the logical flow of the rubric (for example, if the rubric follows the structure of an essay or a report), there are two options: reorder the criteria and note this in the rubric instructions, or add the CLO row at the bottom of the rubric and assign a holistic impression at the point of marking. Part C covers both approaches.

Configuration 3: Unusual or complex rubric compositions

Some rubrics are structured in ways that do not map cleanly to CLOs, for example where a single criterion covers content from multiple CLOs, or where the rubric is highly holistic in nature. In these cases, adding CLO rows at the bottom of the rubric and assigning a holistic impression based on the overall marking pattern is the recommended approach. This preserves the integrity of the existing rubric while still capturing outcome attainment data.

Tip
There is not an unlimited number of ways to configure this. These three configurations cover the vast majority of rubric designs you will encounter. If your rubric does not seem to fit any of them, contact TLI for advice before proceeding.
1
Single Criterion
2
Multiple Criteria
3
Holistic Impression
Step 1

Marking: Single Criterion Mapped to One CLO

This is the most straightforward marking context. You have marked the criterion in the usual way, and the CLO row sits directly beneath it. The task is simply to decide which of the three outcome levels the criterion score corresponds to.

The relationship between criterion score ranges and outcome levels will have been set up when the rubric was designed, but as a general guide:

  • Exceeds expectations: the student's performance on this criterion is at or above the High Distinction or Distinction level descriptor.
  • Meets expectations: the student's performance is at the Credit or Pass level descriptor.
  • Does not meet expectations: the student's performance falls below the Pass level descriptor for this criterion.

Select the corresponding level in the CLO row. This is a quick judgement, and in most cases it will be immediately apparent from the criterion score you have already assigned.

Tip
The CLO row is not a second marking exercise. It is a translation of the judgement you have already made. Do not overthink it. If the student has clearly passed the criterion, they meet expectations. If they have excelled, they exceed. If they have not met the standard, they do not meet.
Step 2

Marking: Multiple Criteria Mapped to One CLO

Where two or more criteria are mapped to a single CLO, you need to make a combined judgement about what the CLO row should reflect. This is an aggregation: you are looking across the group of criteria and asking, taken together, does this student exceed, meet, or not meet this outcome?

In most cases this is a natural and quick call. If all the criteria in the group are clearly at Pass level or above, the outcome is met. If some are at Credit or Distinction and none are below Pass, the outcome is likely met or exceeded. If one or more criteria in the group are below Pass, the outcome is probably not met, though the weighting of those criteria relative to each other may influence the call.

The aggregation does not need to be a precise calculation. It is an informed professional judgement based on the pattern of marks across the grouped criteria, exactly the kind of holistic reading that experienced markers make routinely.

Tip
A useful mental model: imagine a student asking "did I achieve this outcome?" Looking at all the criteria that contribute to it, what would you honestly answer? That answer is your CLO attainment level.
Step 3

Marking: Holistic Impression for Complex Rubrics

Where a CLO row has been placed at the bottom of the rubric because the relevant criteria could not be grouped without disrupting the marking flow, you assign the CLO attainment level based on a holistic reading of the entire submission as it relates to that outcome.

This approach is appropriate when:

  • The criteria contributing to the CLO are spread across the rubric and moving them was not feasible
  • The rubric is highly integrated in nature, making criterion-level attribution difficult
  • The CLO represents a high-level integrative skill (such as critical analysis or professional communication) that is evidenced across the submission as a whole rather than in discrete sections

The holistic impression is not a guess. It is a considered reading of how the student has performed against the outcome across the submission, informed by all the criterion-level marking you have already done. By the time you reach the CLO row at the bottom of the rubric, you will have a clear overall sense of the student's work, and the attainment level will generally be evident.

Tip
If you find yourself genuinely uncertain about a holistic attainment level, that is useful information in itself. It may indicate that the outcome is not well evidenced by the assignment design, which is worth noting for future course iteration.
1
Enable Learning Mastery View
2
Review the Data
3
Export and Send
Step 1

Enable the Learning Mastery Gradebook

The Learning Mastery Gradebook is the view in Canvas that shows CLO attainment data by student. It is not enabled by default and must be switched on manually for each course before you can view or export the data.

To enable it:

  1. Go to Settings in the left-hand course navigation menu.
  2. Click the Feature options tab at the top of the Settings page.
  3. Scroll down the feature list to find Learning Mastery Gradebook, highlighted below.
  4. Toggle the setting to on (the icon on the right will turn to a green tick).
Note
This setting only needs to be enabled once per course. If you can already see "Learning Mastery Gradebook" as an option in your Grades view, it has already been enabled and you can skip this step.
Step 2

Review the Learning Mastery Gradebook

Once the Learning Mastery Gradebook is enabled, navigate to Grades in your course. You will see a dropdown near the top left of the page that allows you to switch between the standard gradebook and the Learning Mastery Gradebook. Select Learning Mastery Gradebook.

This view shows each student as a row and each CLO as a column, with a colour-coded attainment indicator and a numerical score. The colour coding reflects the mastery levels you configured in Part A:

  • Dark green: Exceeds Mastery (Exceeds expectations)
  • Green: Meets Mastery (Meets expectations)
  • Orange: Near Mastery
  • Red: Well Below Mastery (Does not meet expectations)

The top row shows the course average for each CLO, giving you an immediate cohort-level picture of how each outcome is being achieved. This is the data you will share with your program team.

Tip
Before exporting, take a moment to read the cohort-level picture. Are there CLOs where a significant portion of students are orange or red? That is precisely the kind of insight the AoL process is designed to surface. You can act on it now, within the semester, by adjusting your approach in tutorials or directing students to additional resources.
Step 3

Export the Report and Send to Your Program Team

To export the CLO attainment data, click the Export report link in the right-hand panel of the Learning Mastery Gradebook, as shown in the Step 2 screenshot. Canvas will generate and download a CSV file containing the full attainment data for your course.

Once you have the CSV file, send it to your program team or AoL coordinator as directed by your school. This is the data that will be compiled at programme level, mapped to PLOs, and reviewed by the AoL Committee as described in the Data and Reporting section of this hub.

Tip
The CSV file contains student-identifiable data. Send it via a secure channel consistent with the university's data handling requirements. If you are unsure of the appropriate method for your school, check with your program coordinator or TLI.
Key
That is the complete AoL workflow in Canvas. Part A sets up your outcomes. Part B maps them to your rubric. Part C captures attainment at the point of marking. Part D sends the data to the people who can use it to improve the program. Each step is straightforward on its own; together they form a system that makes outcome-level data a natural part of how you already teach and mark.
